coredata.dc:title: A novel electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system of luminol-graphene quantum dot composite and its application in H2O2 detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Kaile
Li, Dejian
Tang, Tingting
Nie, Fei
Zhou, Yang
Du, Jianxiu
Zheng, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: Luminol-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (luminol-NGQDs) nanocomposite was synthesized and a novel electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) process occurred between luminol as the donor and NGQDs as the acceptor in the composite. This ECL-RET effect helped luminol-NGQDs composite produced an anodic ECL signal without coreactants. The ECL-RET mechanism was also studied based on the fluorescence spectra, the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra and the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) spectra. Based on the significant sensitization effect of hydrogen peroxide on luminol-NGQDs ECL signal, an ECL method for the sensitive determination of hydrogen peroxide was established and then applied to the detection of hydrogen peroxide in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot
Luminol
Resonance energy transfer
Hydrogen peroxide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) image of the as prepared luminol-NGQDs. (B) Diameter distribution of luminol-NGQDs.

(A) XPS full survey spectrum of luminol-NGQDs and the corresponding high resolution scans of (B) C1s and (C) N1s spectra. (D) XPS full survey spectrum of NGQDs and the corresponding high resolution scans of (E) C1s and (F) N1s spectra.
The FT-IR spectra of NGQDs, luminol and luminol-NGQDs.

(A) ECL profiles in 0.1 M PBS solutions (pH 9.0) (black line) with4 × 10−5 M NGQDs (green line), luminol (blue line), and luminol-NGQDs (red line), respectively. (B) ECL profiles in 0.1 M PBS (pH 9.0) (black line) with 4 × 10−5 M luminol-NGQDs solution saturated with nitrogen (blue line) or air (red line), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(A) The overlap of UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQDs (green line) and ECL spectrum of luminol (blue line). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra and (C) FL spectra of NGQDs (black line), luminol (red line) and luminol-NGQDs (green line). (D) ECL spectrum of luminol-NGQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

ECL profiles of 4 × 10−5 M luminol-NGQDs in 0.1 M air saturated PBS (pH 9.0) with (red line) or without (black line) 1.0 × 10−5 M hydrogen peroxide. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(A)The ECL profiles of (a) 3.3 × 10−8 M, (b) 8.3 × 10−7 M, (c) 4.2 × 10−6 M, (d) 7.5 × 10−6 M, (e) 2.4 × 10−5 M, (f) 4.1 × 10−5 M, (g) 7.4 × 10−5 M hydrogen peroxide in luminol-NGQDs ECL system. Inset: Calibration curve for hydrogen peroxide determination with a correlation coefficient of 0.9986. (B) Continuous 10 cyclic scans in air saturated PBS (pH 9.0) containing 4 × 10−5 M luminol-NGQDs and 1.0 × 10−5 M hydrogen peroxide.
Results of the hydrogen peroxide determination in water samples.

head.ce:title: A novel electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system of luminol-graphene quantum dot composite and its application in H2O2 detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Analytical Science/Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Science/Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province/School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province/School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminol-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (luminol-NGQDs) nanocomposite was synthesized and a novel electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) process occurred between luminol as the donor and NGQDs as the acceptor in the composite. This ECL-RET effect helped luminol-NGQDs composite produced an anodic ECL signal without coreactants. The ECL-RET mechanism was also studied based on the fluorescence spectra, the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra and the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) spectra. Based on the significant sensitization effect of hydrogen peroxide on luminol-NGQDs ECL signal, an ECL method for the sensitive determination of hydrogen peroxide was established and then applied to the detection of hydrogen peroxide in water samples.

Luminol-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (luminol-NGQDs) nanocomposite was synthesized and a novel electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) process was found between luminol and NGQDs inside the composite. Hydrogen peroxide enhanced luminol-NGQDs ECL signal significantly based on the production of superoxide radical.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is the light-emit process when species generated at electrode via an oxidation or reduction reaction and ECL reagent in an excited state formed consequently. This kind of method has attracted considerable attention due to its inherent advantages such as low background, high sensitivity, and simple instrumentation. Luminol is one of the most famous ECL reagents and can produce an anodic ECL at conventional solid electrodes. Due to the high ECL emission efficiency of luminol, it has been widely used in different analytical application fields [1,2].

Recently, nanomaterials, such as Ag and Au nanoparticle or nanoclusters [3,4], and some semiconductor nanocrystals, known as quantum dots (QDs), like Si [5], CdTe [6] and CdSe QDs [7], were used in ECL analysis owing to their excellent catalytic effect, large specific surface area, and excellent optical properties. Some of them were also used combined with luminol to improve the ECL of luminol [8,9]. Interestingly, a resonance energy transfer (RET) would be occurred between luminol and QDs and some new ECL behaviors would be generated based on this. For example, a stronger ECL signal generated in luminol-CdSe@ZnS QDs ECL-RET system in the neutral condition which was used in the sensing determination of thrombin [10]. The ECL-RET effects of CdS [11], SiO2 [12], CdTe [13] and CdSe/ZnS [14] QDs on luminol ECL were also investigated and some new luminescence properties were obtained by the RET effect. Nevertheless, the inherent biological toxicity of these QDs may lead to serious health and environmental problems since many of these QDs were consisted of heavy metal elements [15]. Therefore, it becomes an urgent challenge for searching the alternative nanomaterials with both ecofriendly and biocompatible to be composited with luminol.

Graphene quantum dot (GQDs), which can be regarded as incredibly small pieces of graphene, were a novel zero-dimensional nanomaterial [16]. Due to its special structure, GQDs exhibited new phenomena by the influence of strong quantum confinement and edge effects. Compared with traditional organic dyes and QDs, GQDs were superior in some unique properties such as low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, good stability and water solubility, easy modification and so on [17–19]. Therefore, it has attracted significant attentions [17–21]. In recent years, doping heteroatom into GQDs was found to improve certain properties of GQDs [22,23]. For example, one-step electrolysis of graphite in sodium p-toluenesulfonate aqueous solution was performed to synthesize sulfur-doped GQDs (SGQDs). Then the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity of the SGQDs were found to be drastically improved compared with GQDs so it exhibited a sensitive response to Fe3+ [24]. Among all of the heteroatom doping studies, many researchers doped nitrogen atoms into GQDs because it can effectively improve the optical properties of GQDs [25,26]. Qu et al. [26] reported a hydrothermal method to prepare nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) using citric acid as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source. The obtained NGQDs gave a blue emission with a high quantum yield about 78%.

In this work, NGQDs was combined with luminol to prepare luminol-NGQDs nanocomposite. Because of the doping of nitrogen atoms, NGQDs exhibited fine luminescence properties and then ECL-RET occurred between luminol as the donor and NGQDs as the acceptor. Moreover, the formation of luminol-NGQDs composite shortened the distance between the two molecules, which facilitated to the generation of RET. Therefore, luminol-NGQDs composite produced an anodic ECL signal without coreactants. The ECL-RET mechanism was also studied based on the fluorescence (FL) spectra, the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and the ECL spectra. Hydrogen peroxide showed a significant sensitization effect on luminol-NGQDs ECL signal. And on this basis, an ECL method for the sensitive determination of hydrogen peroxide was established and the as-proposed ECL method was applied to the detection of hydrogen peroxide in water samples.

Citric acid and urea of analytical reagent grade were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. Luminol (3-aminophtalhydrazide, 98%) was purchased from Aldrich Chemical Company. A luminol stock solution (0.01 M) was obtained by dissolving luminol in 0.1 M NaOH then being stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and prevented from light. Hydrogen peroxide (30%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company and the working solutions were freshly prepared before used. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared with 0.1 M Na2HPO4, 0.1 M NaH2PO4 and 0.1 M KCl and the pH was adjusted by adding a certain concentration of HCl or NaOH to the PBS solution at room temperature (25 °C). All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (> 18 MΩ cm) obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI, USA). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were collected on an AXIS ULTRA instrument (Kratos Analytical, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on the Equinox 55 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on a UV-3310 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence (FL) measurements were carried out on a Tecan infinite M200 Pro multimode reader (Tecan, Switzerland).

The experimental measurements for cyclic voltammetry (CV) and ECL were recorded on a MPI-E multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system (Xi'an Remax Electronic Science &Technology Co. Ltd., Xi’an, China). A conventional three-electrode system was used for electrochemical and ECL measurements. A glassy carbon electrode (GC electrode, 3 mm in diameter) was used as the working electrode, a platinum wire (0.5 mm in diameter) as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl solution) electrode as the reference electrode, respectively. Prior to use, the GC electrode was first polished with sand paper followed by 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 µm alumina slurry, successively. After sonication in ethanol and ultrapure water, the electrodes were rinsed with ultrapure water and allowed to dry at room temperature. The ECL spectra were obtained by collecting the ECL signals during cyclic potential sweep with 8 pieces of cut-off filters of various wavelengths at 400, 412, 425, 440, 460, 475, 490 and 505 nm, respectively.

NGQDs were prepared according to the previous literatures [26]. In brief, 0.21 g (1 mmol) of citric acid and 0.18 g (3 mmol) of urea were dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water and then transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The sealed autoclave was then heated at 160 °C in a furnace for 4 h. The resulting solution was purified by dialysis for 2 days after it was cooled down to room temperature. Then, purified NGQDs were afforded.

Luminol-NGQDs composite was also synthesized. Briefly, 14 mL ethanol/H2O solution (volumetric ratio was 9:5), and 0.33 mL of 1 mg mL−1 NGQDs aqueous solution were placed in a 50 mL beaker flask then fitted with a magnetic stirrer. 0.5 mL of 0.01 M luminol was quickly added to the mixed solution under stirring. After it was reacted for 4 h at room temperature, the resulting solution was purified by dialysis for 2 days. Finally, the collected solution of luminol-NGQDs was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C.

The curves of ECL signal and corresponding cyclic voltammograms (CV) were recorded simultaneously at room temperature in 0.1 M air-saturated PBS (pH 9.0) containing 4 × 10−5 M luminol-NGQDs solution. CV method was used to examine the ECL behavior of luminol-NGQDs system at the GC electrode in the potential range of −0.2 to 0.8 V and CV scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set at −900 V in the process of ECL detection. For hydrogen peroxide ECL detection, hydrogen peroxide solutions with certain concentration were added into the above solution, the enhanced ECL signals were recorded under the same conditions.

The morphologies of luminol-NGQDs composite were characterized by TEM and the surface chemistry of the obtained NGQDs and luminol-NGQDs were characterized by XPS and FT-IR technologies, respectively. Fig. 1 showed the TEM image of luminol-NGQDs and their size distributions, which showed no distinct difference compared with the morphology of NGQDs reported in the literature. As seen from Fig. 1 A, the obtained luminol-NGQDs was rarely aggregated which indicated that the composite has good dispersion ability. And the representative image of individual luminol-NGQDs (Inset in Fig. 1 A) suggesting the high crystallinity of the obtained composite. The size of luminol-NGQDs was distributed in narrow range of 4–7 nm with the average particle size of 5.8 nm (Fig. 1 B).

To research the surface chemical properties of luminol-NGQDs, XPS measurements were employed to examine the element type and chemical state on the surface of luminol-NGQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, XPS full survey spectrum of luminol-NGQDs offered three peaks at 284.8, 399.9 and 531.8 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Fig. 2 B showed luminol-NGQDs C1s spectra and the three peaks located at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.6 eV were assigned to C-C, C-O and O-C˭O species, respectively. In comparison with the C1s spectra of pure NGQDs ( Fig. 2 E), both the decrease of -COOH peak (288.6 eV) and the increase of relative area of C˭C peak (284.8 eV) can be observed, which was believed as the result of the amidation between NGQDs and luminol after the formation of the composite. In addition, Fig. 2 F depicted that there were two peaks at 399.9 eV and 401.1 eV in the luminol-NGQDs N1s XPS spectra. The relative area of the peak at 399.9 eV increased compared with that in the Fig. 2 C, which indicated that luminol has been successfully connected to NGQDs so that amide or amine N added in the luminol-NGQDs composite.

In Fig. 3, it showed the FT-IR spectra of NGQDs, luminol-NGQDs and luminol, respectively. For the FT-IR curve of luminol-NGQDs, a broad peak at about 3500 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of N–H bond in luminol and O–H bond in NGQDs. And a complicated and weak absorption bonds ranging from 1400 to 1600 cm−1 were also observed in the composite according to the stretching vibration of the benzene ring for luminol [27]. In addition, the peak height of a C˭O stretching vibration at 1696 cm−1, especially a C-N stretching vibration at 1200 cm−1 in the spectra of luminol-NGQDs enhanced remarkably compared with the absorption peak of NGQDs, which indicated that the carboxyl group on the surface of NGQDs could react with amino group of the luminol to form a steady acylamino link. Therefore, the results showed that NGQDs and luminol had been composited to form the luminol-NGQDs composite successfully

The ECL behaviors of NGQDs, luminol and luminol-NGQDs were studied comparatively (
Fig. 4 A). When the potential scanned cyclically between −0.2 and 0.8 V, no light emission was detected in blank PBS solution (black curve). Under the same conditions, only one ECL emission peak was observed at the potential of 0.5 V for luminol solution (blue curve), which was attributed to the luminol oxidation in accordance with the previous result [28]. For NGQDs solution, a weak anodic ECL peak was obtained at 0.8 V(green curve), which could resulted from the interaction between QDs and dissolved oxygen [29]. However, the luminol-NGQDs ECL emission was very different (red curve). It produced an ECL emission peak at about 0.7 V with a shoulder ECL peak at 0.5 V. The shoulder peak was attributed to luminol ECL due to the similar ECL emission potential. The maximum emission peak of luminol-NGQDs, which located at 0.7 V, abutted on the emission peak of NGQDs but the ECL intensity of luminol-NGQDs was much high. This indicated that the formation of the luminol-NGQDs composite enhanced the NGQDs ECL signal. The deoxygenation experiment was performed in the luminol-NGQDs solution to evaluate the effect of oxygen. As shown in Fig. 4 B, the ECL intensity decreased greatly under nitrogen-saturated condition, which indicated dissolved oxygen has an enhancement effect on the ECL emission of luminol-NGQDs. As we know [30–32], QDs can receive energy of other luminants at the excited state and then to be excited to produce a luminescence signal subsequently. To further find out whether energy transfer occurred between luminol and NGQDs, UV–vis, FL, and ECL spectra of the luminol-NGQDs system were investigated. In Fig. 5 A, it was found that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQDs overlapped with the ECL spectrum of luminol, which supported that ECL-RET would occur between NGQDs and luminol. In Fig. 5 B, the absorption spectrum of NGQDs showed a peak at 330 nm, and two absorption peaks of luminol were observed at 300 nm and 350 nm, respectively. These were consistent with the results reported in the literature [26,33]. For the absorption spectrum of luminol-NGQDs, only one absorption peak located at about 330 nm was observed, which closed to the adsorption wavelength of NGQDs but showed a slight shift. The change of the absorption peaks suggested that luminol had reacted with NGQDs. The FL spectra in Fig. 5 C showed that the maximum FL emission of NGQDs and luminol were located at 440 nm and 425 nm, respectively. For the luminol-NGQDs composite, it exhibited a maximum FL emission at about 435 nm, which was closer to the maximum emission wavelength of NGQDs. The ECL spectrum of luminol-NGQDs was also investigated in the air-saturated PBS with the potential scanned between −0.2 and 0.8 V. As shown in Fig. 5 D, two ECL emission peaks were observed, suggesting that two kinds of luminophores were generated during the ECL reaction. Compared the ECL spectrum with the FL spectra of luminol, NGQDs and luminol-NGQDs, the emitter of the shoulder ECL peak could be assigned to luminol ECL due to the approximate maximum emission wavelength located at 420 nm. The maximum ECL emission of luminol-NGQDs located at 450 nm was similar with NGQDs FL emission revealing that NGQDs should be the other luminophore in the ECL reaction.

Based on the above results, the mechanism of the luminol-NGQDs anodic ECL signal can be proposed. In the luminol-NGQDs composite, the two units, luminol and NGQDs, can be synchronously oxidized on the GC electrode. In this basic condition, the luminol anions (LH-) was converted into luminol radical (L−•)[28] and NGQDs was converted to NGQDs cations (NGQDs+) after the electrochemical oxidation process. Then redox reaction occurred between L−• and dissolved oxygen in the solution and superoxide radicals (O2 −•) were produced during this process. Subsequently, L−• react with O2 −• and 3-aminophthalate in the excited state (AP*) was generated who released its energy by giving an ECL emission at 420 nm. In the meantime, AP* also gave its energy to NGQDs+ because the overlap of the ECL spectrum of luminol and UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQDs. In the composite, the distance between luminol and NGQDs was so close that the RET process was very easy to implement. This made NGQDs to be excited and an ECL emission with a maximum wavelength at about 450 nm was generated as a result. The ECL-RET mechanism was described in Scheme 1 (Route (1) and (2)).

Hydrogen peroxide could be electro-oxidized to O2 −• on the surface of electrode [29,34], therefore it was considered to enhance the ECL emission of luminol-NGQDs. As expected, it was seen that the anode ECL signal of luminol-NGQDs was significantly enhanced with the addition of hydrogen peroxide (red curve) and its peak potential was almost the same with that of the luminol-NGQDs ( Fig. 6 A). Based on this, a novel method for the determination of hydrogen peroxide was developed and the enhancement mechanism of hydrogen peroxide was also illustrated in the Scheme 1 (Route (1′) and (2′)). Some experimental conditions affecting the ECL intensity were optimized with 0.1 M PBS solution containing 1.0 × 10−5 M hydrogen peroxide, including the concentration of luminol-NGQDs, pH and scan rate. The ECL signal was strongest using 4 × 10−5 M luminol-NGQDs solution (pH 9.0) and the scan rate was 0.1 V/s. Under the above optimal conditions, the relationship between ECL intensity (ΔI = I-I0) and hydrogen peroxide concentration (C) was established, where I0 means the ECL intensity of luminol-NGQDs and I is the ECL intensity of hydrogen peroxide in the luminol-NGQDs ECL system, respectively ( Fig. 7). The ECL intensity increased linearly with the concentration of hydrogen peroxide in the range of 3.3 × 10−8 ~ 7.4 × 10−5 M with the correlation coefficient of 0.9986. The detection limit was calculated at level to 1 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3). As shown in Fig. 7 B, when 10 consecutive potential scans were performed from −0.2 to 0.8 V in PBS solution (pH = 9) containing 1.0 × 10−5 M hydrogen peroxide, no obvious change of the ECL signal was observed and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated as 1.4%, which implies that the method has a good precision. Therefore, the stable ECL signals can be used for the determination of hydrogen peroxide in real samples.

The influence of foreign species was investigated by analyzing a standard solution of 5.0 × 10−5 M hydrogen peroxide to increasing amounts of foreign species. A substance was considered no interference if the variation of the ECL intensity was within ± 5%. The tolerable concentration ratios were finally over 1000-fold for Br-, F-, SO4 2-, NO3 - and CH3COO-, over 300-fold for Mg2+ and NH4 +, and over 30-fold for Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Pb2+. The sensitivity of the present ECL method for hydrogen peroxide determination is relatively high, which detection limit was up to 10 nanomolar level. Therefore, the proposed method has adequate selectivity for the determination of hydrogen peroxide in real samples because the concentrations of the major interfering ions coexisted with hydrogen peroxide in the water samples were about micromolar levels. For the heavy polluted water samples, EDTA or cation exchange resin can be used to remove the interfering ions and then determine the content of hydrogen peroxide with this method.

The local tap water and commercial mineral water were collected. The water samples were directly filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane filter to remove extra species and then be stored at 4 °C. The pH value of water samples was adjusted to 9.0 with 0.1 M NaOH and then hydrogen peroxide in these water samples was detected according to the above ECL method. No hydrogen peroxide was found in these water samples. Then, the spiked water samples were determined and the results were illustrated in Table 1. Recovery tests were carried out to evaluate the accuracy of this method. The recoveries for the three water samples were in the range of 97.4 ~105.2% with the RSD in the range of 1.1–3.9%. Therefore, the method can be used for the determination of hydrogen peroxide in simple water samples with satisfactory accuracy.

A luminol-NGQDs composite was synthesized for the first time. Then, a strong anodic ECL of luminol-NGQDs composite was observed without using additional coreactant. A possible ECL mechanism was briefly discussed. ECL-RET occurred with luminol as energy donor and NGQDs as energy acceptor. Based on the enhancement effect of hydrogen peroxide on the luminol-NGQDs ECL signal, a sensitive ECL method was developed for the determination of hydrogen peroxide. Then the proposed ECL method was successfully used for the determination of hydrogen peroxide in spiked water samples. It promoted for widening the area of ECL-RET analysis and exploring the possibility of employing other ECL system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21305111, 21675124, 21375102), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (No. 2015M572589) and Scientific Research Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory (No. 15JS099).
